questions.question: JavaScript arrays can hold different types of data at the same time.
questions.explanation: Yes, an array can contain strings, numbers, objects, and even other arrays simultaneously.

questions.question: The '===' operator checks for both value and type equality.
questions.explanation: Strict equality (===) ensures that both the value and the data type are identical.

questions.question: The 'DOM' stands for Document Object Model.
questions.explanation: Correct! It represents the structure of an HTML document as a tree of objects.

questions.question: Arrow functions always require the 'function' keyword.
questions.explanation: Arrow functions use the '=>' syntax and do NOT use the 'function' keyword.

questions.question: You can use 'document.getElementById()' to select a class in CSS.
questions.explanation: getElementById() selects by ID. To select by class, use getElementsByClassName() or querySelector().

questions.question: JavaScript is the same as Java.
questions.explanation: They are entirely different languages with different purposes and syntax.
